one.en: PARENTING – INTERIM – Where final hearing for parenting proceedings is listed to commence in June 2026 – Where the parties agreed on a number of issue…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – INTERIM – Where final hearing for parenting proceedings is listed to commence in June 2026 – Where the parties agreed on a number of issues about communication, schooling and travel – Where the applicant seeks interim orders for overnight time – Where the applicant spends extensive daytime with the child – Where the respondent opposes overnight time – Where the respondent alleges unacceptable risk of harm at night from the applicant’s mental health, use of prescribed sedative medication and history of substance abuse – Where the Family Report writer formed a positive view of the applicant’s parenting and found no risk of harm to the child – Where the Family Report writer expressed ongoing alcohol and drug testing of the applicant may have detrimental impacts on the child – Where the applicant provided supportive evidence from his psychiatrist and negative drug test results – Where the Court is satisfied there is no unacceptable risk to the child in the applicant’s care overnight – Where the Court is satisfied it is in the best interests of the child for the applicant to commence modest overnight time with the child.

subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HARPER J:

These are parenting and property proceedings pursuant to Pt VII and Pt VIII of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) between the applicant, Mr Danailova and the respondent, Mr Niemczyk. The child of the relationship is X, born in 2023, currently three years of age.

BACKGROUND

The parties formed a relationship in Country N and were married there in 2021. They separated in May 2024. The child was born by surrogacy.
In 2024, the respondent commenced family law proceedings in the High Court of Country N. The applicant commenced parenting and property proceedings in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) on 29 October 2024. The proceedings were transferred to Division 1 on 4 December 2024.
The case formulated by each party has been summarised by Campton J in Notations to orders made on 25 February 2026 as follows:
The applicant by way of his further Amended Initiating Application filed on 2 February 2026 seeks final orders as to the sharing of long-term decision-making for the child, for the child to live with respondent other than when the child is living with the applicant, and for the child to live with him on a graduated overnight basis during the school-term, together with school holiday time and on special days.
The respondent by way of his further Amended Response to Initiating Application filed on 18 February 2026 seeks sole long-term decision-making in respect of the child, and that he be permitted to remove the child for the purposes of the child living with him in Country N. He seeks orders pending the relocation to Country N for the [applicant] to spend specified time during the day with the child. He does not seek any orders as to the terms of time spent between the applicant and the child subsequent to the relocation.
The parties also have the benefit of a comprehensive report prepared by the single ch 7 parenting expert, Dr E, dated 7 January 2026.
The applicant filed a Further Amended Application in a Proceeding on 12 March 2026 seeking an extensive range of interim parenting orders, about overseas travel, parental responsibility, schooling, spend time arrangements with him, special occasions and orders for spousal maintenance. The respondent sought orders in an Amended Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 11 March 2026, responding to an earlier version of the applicant’s Application in a Proceeding, also seeking a range of orders. These included orders about parental decision making, the child’s spend time arrangements with the applicant, communication, drug testing by the applicant, a psychiatric assessment of the applicant and travel.
The interim dispute was listed for hearing on 13 March 2026. After discussion, many elements of the competing proposals were not pressed. The parties were informed by me that the parenting issues would be listed for hearing as soon as possible on a final basis, with a trial commencing on 29 June 2026. Accordingly, the interim orders made on the basis of these reasons will operate for a relatively short time, that is, up to the delivery of judgment after the trial at the end of June 2026.
No issues concerning forum or arising from the existence of proceedings in Country N are relevant to this interim determination.

CURRENT CIRCUMSTANCES

The applicant lives in Suburb J and the respondent lives in Suburb K. The child currently lives with the respondent but spends daytime of five to six hours, six days per week, with the applicant pursuant to an arrangement agreed between the parties at mediation.
The respondent describes the current arrangements as follows:
[The applicant] spends time with [X] almost every day which has enabled him to build a close relationship with [X] . As was agreed at mediation on 3 August 2024, [the applicant] attends upon my home to collect [X] on a near daily basis, and, provided I observe [the applicant] to be sober and in a good headspace (which so far, I have), [the applicant] will thereafter take [X] out for the agreed period. Upon their return, [the applicant] will spend time with [X], cook his dinner, and attend to his bedtime routine, including putting him to sleep, at times requiring my assistance. [X] often becomes very upset, teary and unsettled and wants to see me before going to sleep. On numerous occasions, I have observed [the applicant] to also fall asleep in [X’s] room and leave my home at around 9:30pm.

(Respondent’s affidavit filed 6 March 2026, paragraph 43)
There was no dispute that under the existing arrangement the child spends extensive periods of time in the sole care of the applicant during the day.

ISSUES

A number of orders were not pressed by either the applicant or respondent, in light of the proposed final hearing dates. Ultimately the issues requiring determination can be summarised as follows:
(a) whether the child should begin spending overnight time with the applicant;

(b) the details of changeover;

(c) whether the applicant should be ordered to undergo further drug testing and a psychiatric assessment; and

(d) some relatively minor disputes relating to travel.

The child has been spending time with the applicant as agreed between the parties, rather than in accordance with any court orders. Each party now seeks court orders prescribing the framework for the child to spend time with the applicant. The parties agree on an interim basis that the child lives with the respondent. The applicant proposes that for three months the child spend time with him each Monday, Thursday and Friday from the end of pre-school until 6.00 pm, each Tuesday from the end of pre-school until the commencement of pre-school on Wednesday, and each Saturday from 8.30 am until 1.30 pm. The applicant then proposes for the next three months that the child stay overnight with him on Tuesday and Thursday, in addition to the daytime hours on Monday and Saturday. The respondent proposes the child spend Monday and Wednesday with the applicant from 1.00 pm until 7.00 pm, and each Tuesday, Thursday, Friday and Saturday from 8.30 am until 1.00 pm.
The parties also agreed on a number of interim orders, including travel outside Australia by the respondent with the child, subject to a minor dispute about the number of days, dealt with below. These will be incorporated into this judgment.

MATERIAL RELIED ON

The applicant relied on the following material as set out in his Case Outline filed on 12 March 2026:
(a) Financial Statement filed 4 July 2025;

(b) Child Impact Report of Dr B filed 3 September 2025;

(c) Single expert report and supplementary report of Mr C filed 30 September 2025;

(d) Affidavit of Mr D filed 8 October 2025;

(e) Family Report of Dr E filed 7 January 2026;

(f) Amended Application for Final Orders filed 2 February 2026;

(g) Affidavit of the applicant filed 13 February 2026;

(h) Further Amended Application in a Proceeding filed 12 March 2026;

(i) Reply affidavit of the applicant filed 10 March 2026; and

(j) Affidavit of Dr G filed 10 March 2026.

The respondent relied on the following documents:
(a) Case Outline filed on 11 March 2026;

(b) Amended Response to Application in a Proceeding filed 22 March 2026;

(c) Affidavit of the respondent filed 6 March 2026; and

(d) Affidavit of Ms H filed 6 March 2026.

The documents tendered and received in to evidence are set out in Schedule 1 to these reasons.

PART VII – PARENTING ORDERS

Orders, including interlocutory orders, in respect of children are made under Pt VII of the Act. The meaning of a “parenting order” is given in s 64B. The Court may make such parenting orders as it thinks proper (s 65D(1)), within the context of the objects of the legislation (s 60B) and subject to s 65DAB.
Section 60B provides that the objects of Pt VII of the Act are to ensure that the best interests of children are met, including by ensuring their safety, and to give effect to the Convention on the Rights of the Child.
When making parenting orders, the Court is required to regard the child’s best interests as the paramount consideration (s 60CA and s 65AA). Section 60CC specifies the criteria which must be considered when determining what form of orders are in the child’s best interests.

Best Interests of the Child

In order to determine the child’s best interests s 60CC of the Act provides that:
(1) Subject to subsection (4), in determining what is in the child’s best interests, the court must:
(a) consider the matters set out in subsection (2); and
(b) ...
For the purposes of s 60CC(1)(a) of the Act the Court must consider the “general considerations” set out under s 60CC(2) which are:
(a) What arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of:
(i) the child; and
(ii) each person who has care of the child (whether or not a person has parental responsibility for the child);
(b) any views expressed by the child;

(c) the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;
(d) the capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

(e) the benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

(f) anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.
Section 60CC(1)(b) and s 60CC(3) of the Act do not apply to the child in these proceedings.
Section 60CC(2A) provides:
(2A) In considering the matters set out in paragraph (2)(a), the court must include consideration of:
(a) any history of family violence, abuse or neglect involving the child or a person caring for the child (whether or not the person had parental responsibility for the child); and

(b) any family violence order that applies or has applied to the child or a member of the child’s family.

Decisions made in interim proceedings are often difficult but are also often a necessary, if temporary, measure until all the evidence can be tested, evaluated and weighed at a final hearing by the making of final parenting orders. So a conservative approach, or one which is likely to avoid harm to a child, is preferred (Marvel & Marvel (No 2) [2010] FamCAFC 101; (2010) 43 Fam LR 348).
An interim parenting hearing is a truncated procedure. The Full Court explained in Goode & Goode [2006] FamCA 1346; (2006) FLC 93-286 at [68]:
... the procedure for making interim parenting orders will continue to be an abridged process where the scope of the enquiry is “significantly curtailed”. Where the Court cannot make findings of fact it should not be drawn into issues of fact or matters relating to the merits of the substantive case where findings are not possible. The Court also looks to the less contentious matters, such as the agreed facts and issues not in dispute and would have regard to the care arrangements prior to separation, the current circumstances of the parties and their children, and the parties’ respective proposals for the future.
Since I am determining an interim application, it is not necessary to discuss every consideration in s 60CC (Tibb v Sheean [2018] FamCAFC 142; (2018) 58 Fam LR 351 at [74]–[88]). What is required is a consideration of the relevant matters which the case presents (Crouper & Mitchell [2014] FamCAFC 246 at [23]). The Full Court explained in Banks & Banks [2015] FamCAFC 36; (2015) FLC 93-637 at [47]–[50] that:
(a) in interim proceedings often only limited consideration may be able to be given to the relevant s 60CC factors;

(b) the issues that are joined will dictate which s 60CC factors are relevant;

(c) the requirement to “consider” each factor does not mean each must be discussed;

(d) where findings made as to some of the s 60CC factors will be determinative of the child’s best interests on an interim basis, it is a sterile and unnecessary exercise to address other factors; and

(e) the trial is the time and place to settle factual controversies.

DISCUSSION

(a) What arrangements would promote the safety (including safety from being subjected to, or exposed to, family violence, abuse, neglect, or other harm) of the child

The safety of a child and the possibility of harm are obviously connected. Recently, in Novakova & Novakova [2026] FedCFamC1A 34 at [24] the Full Court explained:
... The assessment of risk is a predictive exercise based in the evidence, entailing the foresight of possible harm. Risks of harm are not susceptible of scientific demonstration or proof (CDJ v VAJ at [151]), but are instead postulated from known historical facts and present circumstances (Isles & Nelissen [2022] FedCFamC1A 97; (2022) FLC 94-092 at [7]). Possibilities are a legitimate basis for finding that there is such a risk (Malec v JC Hutton Pty Ltd [1990] HCA 20; (1990) 169 CLR 638; Bant & Clayton [2015] FamCAFC 222; (2015) 53 Fam LR 621 at [99]), as long as there exists in the evidence a proper basis for those possibilities.
(Emphasis in original)
The respondent claims there may be a sufficiently serious possibility of harm to the child in the care of the applicant overnight to justify maintaining the existing arrangements until final hearing, that is, refusing any introduction of overnight time. He argued that the child is flourishing under the existing arrangements, and nothing should be done by the Court to disrupt this positive trajectory. Consequently, the claim of risk was limited to potential overnight time. The respondent was driven to argue the risk arose, or at least became unacceptable, only at night. This was inevitable when the existing arrangements place the child in the applicant’s care for up to six hours for six days each week during the day.
The assessment of whether or not a risk is “unacceptable” is a judgmental step which does not easily admit of elaboration (Eastley & Eastley [2022] FedCFamC1A 101; (2022) FLC 94-094 at [39] citing Athens v Randwick City Council [2002] NSWCA 83 at [16]).
The respondent argues that there is sufficient evidence at an interim stage to support a prediction of risk of harm. He acknowledged both parties have had a problem with substance abuse in the past, as recorded by Dr E. He gave evidence claiming he has conquered his own addictive patterns of behaviour.
However, he claims the applicant has a history of volatile emotional responses, mood swings and lack of regulation. He relied upon an affidavit of Ms H filed 6 March 2026 which referred to the applicant creating a toxic workplace in about 2019, some seven years ago. He pointed to the applicant’s diagnosis of Major Depressive Disorder and Generalised Anxiety Disorder which constitutes a serious mental health condition. He claimed the applicant trivialised the seriousness of this condition. He points to the use by the applicant of sedative medications at night, and alleges he has been resistant to giving full disclosure about his consultations with health professionals over the last three years. It was the tenor of this argument that in those circumstances the Court should conclude at an interim hearing that whatever capacity the applicant has during the daylight hours to regulate his mood, behaviour and any addictive impulses, will falter or dissipate when the sun sets, placing the child at the risk of harm. I find this argument unconvincing.
Many of the matters relied upon by the respondent are contentious and cannot be resolved at an interim hearing. But in any event, taking a cautious approach and looking to undisputed and less contentious facts, none of the matters pointed to by the respondent give sufficient support to an inference of risk in the applicant’s overnight care, particularly when taken with the expert evidence of Dr E, discussed below. The evidence of Ms H had no probative value at an interim stage, despite being read without objection.
The respondent sought to bolster his argument by pointing to the absence of any recent drug testing from the applicant. But the applicant underwent and provided drug test results from 26 November 2024 and 17 September 2025, as well as hair follicle testing results from 3 December 2024. These all gave negative results for the presence of any drug other than benzodiazepine, which has been prescribed for the applicant to address sleeping difficulties. The respondent made the submission that the applicant has “a tendency to relapse” and has provided no drug testing since September 2025. As a result, so the argument goes, the respondent cannot be reassured that the applicant has not relapsed into earlier drug abuse, despite conceding his own observations of the applicant have shown him to be “sober and in a good headspace”, when delivering the child to the applicant’s home.
These arguments are also unconvincing. They are the same as arguing that the Court should assume a proclivity to relapse on the part of the applicant and conclude his existing and relatively recent drug test results, which are undisputed evidence, demonstrate little if nothing and provide no basis for an interim inference that the applicant remains drug free. It also implies that in the absence of the applicant providing continual drug testing, an interim assumption of risk of possible relapse should be made. This argument is made in circumstances where the respondent argues his own assertion of remaining drug free, unsupported by any testing, should simply be taken at face value at an interim stage, while the applicant is obliged to continually prove a negative test result to the respondent’s satisfaction.
The applicant’s use of sedative medication at night provides insufficient support for a conclusion of unacceptable risk at an interim stage. The respondent argued there should be a professional assessment of the effect of the medication upon the applicant, but the medication is prescribed and, according to Dr G, the applicant’s treating Psychiatrist, is well tolerated by the applicant.
It was apparent from the submissions of the respondent that he tended to conflate his own stated desires for reassurance about the stability, drug use, mental health and parenting capacity of the applicant with the best interests of the child. Clearly a parent’s desire for reassurance cannot of itself be treated as determinative of the best interests of the child, although I accept anxiety about the child’s possible exposure to harm with one parent can have an impact on the other parent’s capacity. In this case the submission was made that the current arrangements mitigate the risk the respondent’s concerns might be exacerbated if he did not have the opportunity to make observations of the capacity of the applicant. However, as mentioned above, the respondent gave evidence of his own observations of the applicant, which were positive, and the current arrangements also involve daytime periods during which there is no observation of the applicant.
It is relevant to point out that the respondent expressed the same concerns to Dr E, which she recorded at paragraph 60 of her report:
[The respondent] expressed a number of concerns regarding [the applicant’s] parenting capacity, most of which centred on his perception of [the applicant’s] inconsistency, emotional volatility, and the risk of a return to past patterns of behaviour. He said he worries that, without the current external supports in place, [the applicant] may eventually revert to taking illicit drugs, particularly given the history of substance use that existed earlier in their relationship. While he acknowledged that recent drug testing does not indicate any current use, he said he remained apprehensive about the future, as he perceived illicit drugs to have been closely tied to periods of unhappiness and instability for [the applicant] in the past.
Dr E considered these concerns and had access to clinical notes produced by Dr G. Despite the expressed concerns of the respondent, Dr E formed a positive view of the applicant’s parenting capacity as an “exceptionally thoughtful and intentional parent” (see further below under s 60CC(d)).
Dr E also stated at paragraph 78 of her report that “[t]here is no evidence to suggest that X has been at risk in the care of either parent. He appears to love both parents deeply, and each provides him with warmth, affection, and consistent caregiving”.
At paragraph 80 of her report, Dr E opined:
In evaluating [X’s] safety, I have considered the affidavit material, my observations of [X] with each parent, the historical therapeutic records, and the parents’ respective accounts during interview. Nothing in the material suggests that [X] is at risk of harm while in the direct care of either parent. Both [the applicant] and [the respondent] demonstrate strong parental capacity, emotional warmth, and developmentally attuned caregiving. [X’s] presentation as a confident, social, articulate and emotionally regulated toddler indicates that he has benefited from safe, nurturing and responsive parenting from both his parents.
The respondent argued this opinion should be treated with caution, because a transition to overnight time would likely heighten conflict between the parents, which is a main source of risk to the child (as Dr E noted, see below at [47]). This argument is also unpersuasive. It assumes the child’s positive presentation may be undermined if modest interim overnight time with the applicant is ordered and if the respondent loses the opportunity to have oversight of the applicant’s interactions with the child. Dr E has clearly identified the parties’ failure to collaborate as the central problem. The respondent’s argument assumes ongoing or escalating conflict in the face of knowing this expert opinion. While such an outcome is possible, I consider it unlikely and it would reflect very poorly on the parties in the lead up to a final hearing, and in the orders of 13 February 2026 a parenting co-ordinator was appointed which is likely to assist in resolving or ameliorating conflict.
Bearing in mind the truncated nature of an interim hearing, I am not persuaded on the basis of undisputed evidence or less contentious matters that the child would be at any risk of harm, and certainly any unacceptable risk, in some limited overnight care with the applicant.

(b) Any views held by the child:

The child is too young to express useful views.

(c) The developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child;

Dr E expressed the following views about the developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs of the child as follows:
At two years of age, [X] requires predictable routines, consistent caregiving responses, and opportunities to develop independence, socialisation, and language. Both parents demonstrate awareness of these developmental needs. [The applicant] has engaged extensively with early childhood literature and [...] parenting approaches, integrating these into daily routines such as self-directed feeding, language expansion, and structured opportunities for independent exploration. [The respondent] demonstrates a strong emphasis on stability, emotional security, and exposure to positive adult role models within the home. Both parents provide environments in which [X] experiences affection, attuned communication, and emotional containment.
[X’s] developmental trajectory is very positive. He has advanced expressive language, excellent self-regulation, and a curious and observant temperament. As an only child, his need for peer socialisation will become increasingly important. Both parents recognise that he will require structured preschool education when he turns three.
Clearly the child is developing well. The respondent used this fact to argue that the Court should do nothing to interfere with this by changing the existing arrangements. I have considered this argument closely.

(d) The capacity of each person who has or is proposed to have parental responsibility for the child to provide for the child’s developmental, psychological, emotional and cultural needs;

Dr E formed the view that both parents have a good parenting capacity:
Both parents possess substantial strengths in their capacity to meet [X’s] developmental and emotional needs. [The applicant] presents as an exceptionally thoughtful and intentional parent who has invested time in understanding toddler development and who demonstrates very strong attunement to [X’s] cues. His caregiving is patient, organised and child-centred. He has been integrally involved in [X’s] daily routines since birth, which was evident throughout the therapeutic and affidavit material. He also demonstrates insight into the importance of structure, autonomy, and consistent boundaries.
[The respondent] shows a deep commitment to providing [X] with security, routine, stability and predictable adult relationships. He has fostered a home environment characterised by warmth, safety and positive social modelling. He engages [X] in imaginative play, language-rich interactions, and gentle parental guidance. His interactions during observation were affectionate, confident and responsive.
The primary difficulty lies not in the parenting capacity of either parent, but in their limited ability to collaborate. Both parents have valid perspectives, yet they struggle to reconcile these views, resulting in unilateral decision-making around issues such as feeding approaches, toilet training, and early education. If unresolved, these inconsistencies may, in time, lead to confusion for [X], particularly as he enters a developmental stage characterised by rapidly increasing social and cognitive awareness.
[X] is securely and lovingly attached to both parents, and maintaining these relationships is essential to his emotional wellbeing. He clearly experiences both men as important attachment figures and benefits from different but complementary forms of parenting. His happy disposition, confident exploratory behaviour, advanced language skills, and his capacity to engage with both parents in imaginative, reciprocal play all reflect the emotional security he derives from each of them. ...
I have taken this evidence into account.

(e) The benefit to the child of being able to have a relationship with the child’s parents, and other people who are significant to the child, where it is safe to do so;

There was no dispute that X would benefit from a meaningful relationship with each parent. As pointed out, the respondent proposes on a final basis that he be permitted to relocate to Country N with the child. At final hearing an assessment of the nature and depth of the meaningful relationship with the applicant and its benefits to the child are likely to be informed by the attachment that X further develops with the applicant in the weeks or months before a determination of the respondent’s application for relocation to Country N. This consideration militates in favour of ordering some overnight time with the applicant.

(f) Anything else that is relevant to the particular circumstances of the child.

The respondent contends that orders should be made for a psychiatric assessment of the applicant. In submissions, he also offered to undergo such an assessment himself.
The discussion above under s 60CC(2)(a), and the opinions of Dr E about the excellent parenting capacity of both parents militate against the need for any such assessments.
The pressure from the respondent for ongoing drug and alcohol testing, as well as a psychiatric assessment may have adverse impacts on the child. Dr E pointed to detrimental impacts on the child posed by ongoing alcohol and drug testing in the absence of evidence of risk:
The requirement for ongoing alcohol and drug testing, alongside behavioural restrictions applied solely to [the applicant], is likely to further shape the dynamics of the co-parenting relationship in a way [X] may come to perceive. These conditions, without supporting evidence that [the applicant] poses any current risk, imply deficit, danger, and mistrust. Over time, children can internalise these relational cues, especially where one parent’s authority is consistently subordinated to the other’s. ...
I am not inclined to make interim orders for either drug and alcohol testing or psychiatric assessment of either party.

Time with the applicant

Dr E counselled against a “sudden or rapid” material shift away from the current arrangements. I am satisfied that it is in the best interests of the child for his time with the applicant to be increased on an interim basis to include each Tuesday night in the care of the applicant. In light of the trial dates at the end of June 2026, I consider it desirable to otherwise broadly maintain the existing arrangements, based upon the proposed orders of the applicant.
For some reason, there was no agreement about changeover. For the purposes of interim arrangements, I will adopt the applicant’s proposed order.

Overseas travel

Overseas travel was substantially agreed. The applicant consented to the respondent’s travel to Country F and Country N with the child. This travel will take place before the final hearing at the end of June 2026.
Although there was agreement about providing a bond of $100,000, the parties remained in dispute about the number of days during which the respondent may travel outside Australia with the child. The applicant proposed no more than 14 days outside Australia, the respondent wanted 18 days. I would allow 18 days. There is no sensible basis in the evidence at an interim hearing to prefer one over the other, other than the agreement to permit the respondent’s travel to Country N, which supports the longer period. In any event, the limitation to 14 or 18 days is largely otiose in light of the agreement for the respondent to travel prior to final hearing, so I see no need to resolve this difference at an interim stage.
The parties also could not agree upon the time to elapse before the applicant could draw upon the bond provided by the respondent. I will allow 28 days.

Mediation

The parties agreed to an order that they engage in a lawyer assisted mediation within 12 weeks of the interim hearing.

COSTS

Each party sought costs. I will make no order as to costs of the interlocutory applications determined by this judgment.

CONCLUSION

For the foregoing reasons, the orders at the commencement of this judgment will be made.
